what_i_noticed: 1. test_monotonic_horizons was failing with 'AttributeError: LogisticRegression has no attribute multi_class' — a sklearn version mismatch (model trained with sklearn 1.8.0, env has 1.4.0). 2. MC drift errors of 10-57% reported in data — but these are measured by the data_generator's own separate GBM, not the backend MC. The data_generator's quality check formula compares mu*T (arithmetic) against (mu-0.5*sigma^2)*T (geometric), inflating the reported error. 3. In the backend's MC, Student-t(df=8) innovations have Var=df/(df-2)=1.333, adding ~33% excess diffusion variance vs the assumed unit variance, biasing simulated price paths upward.
hypothesis: 1. The crash model's LogisticRegression objects loaded from a sklearn 1.8.0 pickle are missing the 'multi_class' attribute that sklearn 1.4.0's predict_proba requires. Patching this attribute on load should fix the test. 2. Normalizing Student-t innovations to unit variance will remove the excess diffusion variance bias in the backend's MC paths, preserving fat tails (kurtosis) while correcting the drift.
what_i_did: 1. In crash_model.py load_model(): added a compatibility patch that sets lr_model.multi_class='auto' on any loaded LogisticRegression object missing that attribute. Also added a try/except fallback in predict_proba so LR failures degrade gracefully to LGB-only predictions. 2. In monte_carlo.py simulate_paths(): after generating Student-t innovations, normalized them by dividing by sqrt(df/(df-2)) when df>2, ensuring unit variance while preserving the tail shape.
analysis: The crash model test fix is definitive — the root cause was a sklearn version incompatibility, and the patch is forward/backward compatible. The Student-t normalization is mathematically sound: raw Student-t(8) has Var=8/6=1.333, which inflates the diffusion term by ~33%, adding ~0.5*sigma^2*0.333 excess annual drift to price paths. This is most impactful for high-vol stocks (TSLA, BA at ~40-50% vol: ~3-4% annual excess). The data_generator's drift error metrics are NOT affected by this fix since it runs its own separate GBM. Note: the data_generator's drift_error formula itself has a measurement bias (compares mu*T against (mu-0.5*sigma^2)*T), inflating reported errors — but this cannot be fixed per lab/ read-only constraint.
next_steps: 1. Consider adding a variance-drag Ito correction (-0.5*sigma_t^2*dt) to the MC drift when base_drift is intended as the TARGET ARITHMETIC return rather than log return — the current code's comment says it's log return but run_monte_carlo passes log(1+arithmetic_return). 2. Investigate why cross-asset correlations show 0 observations (n_observations=0). 3. Look into the block bootstrap code path — it's enabled in config but never activated since historical_residuals is never passed to simulate_paths from run_monte_carlo.
